planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist to average, deep loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Engelmann spruce grows naturally at high elevations (6,000–12,000 feet) in the Rocky Mountains and requires cool, moist conditions — it does not tolerate heat, drought, or alkaline soils well.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist to average, deep loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Engelmann spruce is the dominant conifer of the subalpine Rocky Mountain forests — the tree that defines the timberline forest from Colorado to British Columbia, often co-occurring with subalpine fir (Abies lasiocarpa) in the classic subalpine parkland just below treeline. The blue-gray to dark blue-green needles are sharp (typical of all spruces) and the crown is narrowly conical with pendulous lower branches. Engelmann spruce wood is valued as a tonewood for acoustic guitar tops, violin tops, and piano soundboards — the finest-grained specimens produce some of the best tonewood available. More cold-tolerant than Colorado blue spruce but generally less drought-tolerant. Best in cool, high-elevation or northern garden sites in zones 2–6 where the cool, moist conditions of its natural habitat can be approximated.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Korean fir prefers moist, cool conditions and does not tolerate hot, dry summers well. Best in zones 5–7 with cool, moist summers. More compact and ornamental than most native firs.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Korean fir is the premier ornamental fir for garden use, valued above all other firs for a remarkable trait — it begins producing cones at a very young age (sometimes at 5–10 years old), and the upright cones are extraordinarily beautiful, bright purple-violet fading through blue to brown as they mature, prominently displayed on upright branches. The short stature compared to native firs makes it manageable in residential gardens. Cultivated varieties include 'Silberlocke' (with upturned needles that display their white undersides), 'Horstmann's Silberlocke' (more compact), 'Kohout's Ice Breaker' (very compact), and many others. Native to the mountains of South Korea. The compact size, precocious cone production, and beautiful cone color make this the most horticulturally valuable of all the firs for home gardens.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in full sun; deodar cedar is the most heat-tolerant of the true cedars and the standard large ornamental conifer of the American South and West. Excellent drainage is essential — will not survive in wet, poorly drained soils.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average loamy or sandy soil in full sun; deodar cedar is the standard large landscape conifer of the American South, widely planted as a specimen and shade tree across the Southeast and Pacific Coast in zones 7–9. The graceful, weeping branch tips and the soft, blue-green needles in clusters of 20–30 create one of the most elegant of all large conifers — the drooping leading shoot and pendulous branch tips are immediately distinctive. Native to the western Himalayas of Pakistan, Afghanistan, and India, where it is a sacred tree — 'deodar' means 'divine tree' in Sanskrit. Grows rapidly (2–4 feet per year) in good conditions. Makes an outstanding specimen for large lawns where it develops its full, sweeping canopy. The golden-yellow cultivar 'Gold Cone' is striking. More heat-tolerant than the other true cedars, making it the best choice for the Southeast.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, average to lean soil in full sun; Atlas cedar is tolerant of drought and lean soils once established — native to the Atlas Mountains of Morocco and Algeria where it grows on dry, rocky limestone slopes. Excellent drainage is critical; wet, heavy clay soil causes rapid decline.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, average to lean soil in full sun; Atlas cedar is prized above all other true cedars for the intense blue-silver to blue-gray needle color of its most striking forms — the blue cultivars, collectively sold as 'Glauca', are among the bluest conifers in cultivation, with a color intensity rivaling or exceeding Colorado blue spruce. The stiff, ascending branches and flat-topped, wide-spreading crown of mature trees develop over decades into magnificent architectural specimens. The weeping cultivar 'Glauca Pendula' is a spectacular accent plant with long, cascading, blue branches that can be trained over walls, pergolas, or staked to create dramatic weeping specimens. More drought-tolerant than deodar cedar once established, native to the semi-arid Atlas Mountain range. The large, barrel-shaped, upright cones take two years to mature. Hardy to zone 6 — more cold-tolerant than deodar cedar.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), lean, sandy or gravelly, peaty soil in full sun; Scotch heather requires extremely acidic, nutrient-poor, well-drained soil and full sun — it fails quickly in fertile, neutral, or alkaline soil or in shade. Do not fertilize.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), lean, sandy or gravelly, peaty soil in full sun; Scotch heather is the defining plant of the British moorlands — the vast open landscapes of Scotland, northern England, Wales, and Ireland carpeted with purple heather in August are among the most iconic and beloved landscapes in Europe. The genus Calluna contains only this single species, distinguished from the related Erica heathers by having the colored sepals (not petals) as the main display. Hundreds of cultivars exist in shades from white through pink, lavender, and deep purple, as well as double-flowered and golden-foliaged forms. Shear lightly after flowering to maintain compact, bushy plants — unsheared plants become leggy and open. Invaluable for dry, acidic, heathland-style gardens in appropriate climates. Late-summer (August) bloom provides nectar for migrating monarch butterflies, honeybees, and native bees during the fall migration period.
